questions.optionB: Complete destruction or removal of ALL forms of microbial life, including spores
questions.explanation: Sterilization: complete elimination of ALL microbial life including bacterial spores (most resistant). Methods: autoclaving (121°C, 15 psi, 15 min — most reliable, uses moist heat under pressure), hot air oven (160°C, 1 hour — dry heat), radiation (gamma — disposables), filtration (heat-sensitive solutions), ethylene oxide (gas — heat/moisture sensitive items). Disinfection: kills vegetative forms but NOT necessarily spores. Pasteurization: reduces pathogen load (does not sterilize). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Sterilization.

questions.questionText: Autoclave operates on the principle of:
questions.optionB: Moist heat under pressure (121°C, 15 psi, 15 minutes)
questions.explanation: Autoclave: gold standard of sterilization. Uses saturated steam under pressure → moist heat at 121°C (15 psi) for 15 minutes. Kills all organisms including spores (by denaturing proteins and nucleic acids). Used for: culture media, surgical instruments, dressings, liquids. Spore test (biological indicator): Geobacillus stearothermophilus (used to validate autoclave function). Chemical indicators: autoclave tape (color change). Cannot autoclaving: powders, oils, sharp instruments. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Autoclave.

questions.optionB: Establish the causal relationship between a specific microorganism and a specific disease
questions.explanation: Koch's postulates (1884): (1) Organism must be found in all cases of the disease (not in healthy), (2) Organism must be isolated from diseased host in pure culture, (3) Pure culture must reproduce the disease when inoculated into susceptible host, (4) Organism must be re-isolated from experimentally infected host. Limitations: viruses (no pure culture on media), obligate intracellular organisms, carriers, ethical concerns. Molecular Koch's postulates (Falkow): use molecular techniques for non-cultivable organisms. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Koch's Postulates.

questions.questionText: Gram staining differentiates bacteria based on:
questions.optionB: Cell wall composition — Gram-positive (thick peptidoglycan, teichoic acids, retain crystal violet/purple) vs Gram-negative (thin PG, outer membrane with LPS, appear pink/red)
questions.explanation: Gram stain (Hans Christian Gram, 1884): most important staining technique. Steps: (1) Crystal violet (primary stain), (2) Gram's iodine (mordant — CV-iodine complex), (3) Decolorizer (acetone/alcohol — critical step), (4) Safranin (counterstain). Gram-positive: thick peptidoglycan layer retains CV-iodine → PURPLE. Gram-negative: thin PG, outer membrane dissolved by decolorizer → loses CV → counterstained PINK/RED by safranin. LPS (endotoxin): Gram-negative outer membrane. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Gram Stain.

questions.questionText: Endotoxin (LPS) differs from exotoxin in that endotoxin:
questions.optionB: Is lipopolysaccharide (Lipid A component), is part of Gram-negative cell wall, is heat stable, and causes fever/shock/DIC
questions.explanation: Endotoxin (LPS): integral part of Gram-negative outer membrane. Lipid A: toxic moiety. Heat stable (survives autoclaving). Released on bacterial lysis. Effects: fever (via IL-1/TNF), septic shock (vasodilation, hypotension), DIC, NO formation. Cannot be converted to toxoid. Limulus Amebocyte Lysate (LAL) test for detection. Exotoxins: proteins, secreted, heat labile, highly specific, potent, can be toxoided (formaldehyde treatment → vaccine, e.g., tetanus toxoid, diphtheria toxoid). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Endotoxin vs Exotoxin.

questions.questionText: The most common method of gene transfer in bacteria that involves cell-to-cell contact is:
questions.optionB: Conjugation (via sex pilus/F pilus — transfers plasmid DNA)
questions.explanation: Bacterial gene transfer: (1) Conjugation: direct cell-cell contact via sex pilus (F pilus) → transfer of F plasmid or chromosomal DNA. Most common mechanism for antibiotic resistance transfer. Requires F+ (donor) and F− (recipient). (2) Transformation: uptake of free/naked DNA from environment (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae — Griffith's experiment). (3) Transduction: bacteriophage-mediated DNA transfer. Generalized: random DNA. Specialized: specific genes (e.g., diphtheria toxin gene by β-prophage). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Gene Transfer.

questions.optionB: Clostridium (anaerobic) and Bacillus (aerobic) — only Gram-positive bacilli form endospores
questions.explanation: Endospores: highly resistant dormant structures formed by ONLY Gram-positive bacilli — Clostridium (strict anaerobe) and Bacillus (aerobe/facultative). Resistant to heat, desiccation, chemicals, radiation. Dipicolinic acid + calcium: key spore component (heat resistance). Clostridium species: C. tetani (drumstick spore/terminal), C. botulinum (subterminal), C. perfringens (subterminal), C. difficile. Bacillus: B. anthracis (central, non-bulging), B. cereus. Spores: NOT killed by boiling (need autoclaving). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Bacterial Spores.

questions.questionText: Biofilm formation is clinically significant because biofilms:
questions.optionB: Provide protection from antibiotics and host immunity, causing chronic persistent infections on medical devices
questions.explanation: Biofilms: structured communities of bacteria enclosed in self-produced extracellular polymeric matrix (polysaccharides, proteins, DNA) on surfaces. Clinical significance: (1) Extremely resistant to antibiotics (1000x MIC), (2) Protected from phagocytosis, (3) Cause chronic infections (catheter-associated UTI, prosthetic joint infections, endocarditis, ventilator-associated pneumonia, dental plaque). Common biofilm formers: S. aureus, S. epidermidis, Pseudomonas, Candida. Treatment often requires device removal. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Biofilms.
